channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Goal
item.description: A goal is a directional ambition that orients work without being reducible to a single decidable target. It names what success looks like in broad terms and supplies the orientation that objectives, projects, and plans translate into decidable and actionable structure. “Modernize the records management system,” “pursue justice,” “build a team that can sustain this work after us” — each names a direction that organizes effort but is not itself a yes/no question.

An adjustment is a change made in response to a finding. It is what the feedback stage produces. An adjustment takes a finding (something we learned from review) and produces a concrete change to an entity in the system — a plan, a process, a term, a policy, an office definition.

¶What an adjustment carries

  • responds-to — links to the finding(s) this adjustment addresses. An adjustment without a finding is an ungrounded change. A finding without an adjustment is a lesson ignored.
  • target — what entity is being changed. A plan being revised, a process being tightened, a term being sharpened, a policy being added, an office’s capability signature being updated. The target is always a specific entity, not a vague area.
  • change — what specifically is different. Not “improve the dispatch process” but “add a capacity check predicate to office entities so dispatch can verify bandwidth before assigning.” The change is the ground term — fully specified.
  • rationale — why this change addresses the finding. The link from cause (in the finding) to fix (in the adjustment). If the finding’s cause was “agents lack capacity information at dispatch time,” the rationale is “adding capacity as a predicate makes it available to any composition that includes the office entity.”

¶Adjustments close the loop

Deming’s PDCA cycle (1986) calls the feedback step “Act” — but Act does not mean “do more work.” It means “adjust the system based on what Check revealed.” An adjustment is not new work. It is a modification to the conditions under which future work happens. The distinction matters: new work enters through planning. Adjustments modify what planning, dispatch, execution, tracking, and review do next time.

A finding is a judgment comparing what happened to what was supposed to happen. It is what the review stage produces. A finding takes observations (actual state) and plan/goal predicates (intended state) as inputs and produces a structured assessment: what the gap is, why it exists, and how much it matters.

¶What a finding carries

  • observed — links to the observations this finding is based on. What actually happened, as measured by tracking.
  • expected — links to the plan, goal, or objective that defined what should have happened. The criterion against which actuality is judged.
  • variance — the gap between observed and expected. Not just “different” but how different and in what direction. “Planned 5 completions, observed 2” is a variance. “Completed on time but the output doesn’t meet the definition-of-done” is also a variance.
  • cause — the systemic factor explaining the variance. Following Dekker (2006): the question is never “who is responsible?” but “what conditions made this outcome likely?” A finding’s cause points at system structure, not at agents. Missing information, unclear procedures, tool failures, capacity overload, wrong assumptions in the plan.
  • scope — whether this is single-loop or double-loop (Argyris and Schön, 1978). A single-loop finding says “execution deviated from plan” — the fix is tactical. A double-loop finding says “the plan was wrong” — the fix is structural. The distinction matters because they feed different kinds of adjustments.
  • severity — how much this finding matters. A minor variance on a low-priority task is noise. A systemic factor that affects every plan is critical. Severity determines which findings get acted on first.

¶The audit finding structure

The International Standards of Supreme Audit Institutions formalize audit findings as four components: condition (what is), criteria (what should be), cause (why the gap), effect (what results from the gap). This maps directly: condition = observed, criteria = expected, cause = cause, effect = the downstream impact that severity measures.

An observation is a derived reading of system state. It is what the tracking stage produces. An observation takes events and entity states as input and produces a computed quantity — a metric, an anomaly, a status snapshot.

Observations are not raw data. Raw data is the event stream — timestamped records of acts. An observation is a derivation from that stream: “plan X is 3 of 7 tasks complete” requires reading seven task entities and computing a ratio. “Velocity this sprint was 5 tasks” requires counting completion events within a time window. The observation records both the value and the method that produced it, so the derivation is reproducible.

A record is a committed artifact of organizational activity. Plans, tasks, decisions, analyses, ideas, projects, goals, objectives, milestones, reviews — all are records. What makes something a record: it was committed (someone created it deliberately), it persists (it’s a file, not a conversation), and it carries provenance (who created it, when, serving what purpose).

Records form the operational memory of the organization. A decision record captures a choice with its context and rationale. A plan record captures an approach with its tasks and dependencies. A task record captures a unit of work with its goal and completion criteria. Each specialization adds predicates specific to its kind of work, but all share the base record predicates: id, type, status, date-created.

item.title: Dispatch
item.description:
Dispatch is the stage of the operations loop where work meets worker. A task exists in a plan. An office exists with capabilities and authority. Dispatch creates the assignment that connects them.

¶The three checks

Every dispatch act checks three conditions before creating an assignment (Gerkey and Matarić, 2004):

  1. Capability — the agent CAN do this type of work. The role’s capability signature matches the task’s requirements. In fiber terms: respond(office, task) produces t on the capability predicates.

item.title: Execution
item.description:
Execution is the stage of the operations loop where work actually happens. A task has been assigned to an agent. Execution is the sequence of acts that advance the task’s state from assigned toward completed.

¶What execution produces

Execution produces three things:

  1. State changes — the task moves through states (assigned → in-progress → completed, or → blocked, or → failed). Each transition is an act that changes the work entity’s state.

item.title: Feedback
item.description:
Feedback is the stage that closes the operations loop. Review identifies what went as planned and what didn’t. Feedback acts on those findings — modifying future plans, adjusting processes, updating policies, creating new vocabulary.

Without feedback, the system runs open-loop: it plans, dispatches, executes, tracks, and reviews, but the review findings go nowhere. The next cycle starts from the same assumptions. The same mistakes are made. The same gaps persist. Feedback is what makes the loop a LOOP rather than a line.

item.title: Planning
item.description:
Planning is the stage of the operations loop where goals become structured commitments. A goal says what direction to move. Planning says what steps to take, in what order, with what dependencies. The output is a plan — a container of tasks connected by a dependency graph.

¶What planning produces

Plans. Not documents ABOUT plans — the plan entity IS the plan. Its frontmatter carries the goal, the task list, the ordering, the definition-of-done. The plan’s structure is the plan’s content.

item.title: Tracking
item.description:
Tracking is the stage of the operations loop where the system observes itself. Execution produces events — timestamped records of acts. Tracking reads those events, computes derived quantities (metrics), and surfaces anomalies.

¶What tracking produces

  1. Status snapshots — the current state of every work entity, derived from the event stream
  2. Metrics — derived quantities like velocity, throughput, cycle time, WIP (Little, 1961)
  3. Anomalies — deviations from expected patterns: stale tasks, blocked plans, capacity overruns, drift

¶What tracking does NOT do

Tracking does not act on what it observes. It does not reassign work, escalate blockers, or adjust plans. Those are acts in the dispatch, review, and feedback stages. Tracking’s separation from action is structural — an observer that also modifies what it observes has a conflict of interest.

item.title: Blocked
item.description:
Blocked is the state of a unit of work that cannot proceed. Something it depends on is not yet met — another task is incomplete, a resource is unavailable, an external condition prevents progress.

Blocked can follow either open (a task that was available but became blocked when a dependency regressed) or in-progress (work that was underway but hit an obstacle). It precedes open — when the blocking condition resolves, the task returns to open. The transition happens when the dependency is met, which is itself a settlement event: the depended-on entity reaching completed settles the blocker.

item.title: Campaign
item.description:
A campaign is a time-bounded, coordinated push across multiple projects and plans toward a strategic intermediate objective. It is the organizing concept between project (a container of plans toward one goal) and enterprise (the indefinite aim-generative activity). A campaign says: “for this period, these projects and plans are coordinated toward THIS objective, after which the landscape is different enough that the next campaign can begin.”

¶What makes something a campaign

Four properties distinguish a campaign from a project or a plan:

item.title: Completed
item.description:
Completed is the terminal state of successful work. The task’s goal has been achieved. No further action is needed or possible on this unit of work.

When a task reaches completed, the part-of relation entails consequences for the whole: a completed part contributes complete to its container. When ALL parts of a plan are completed, the plan’s state is constituted as completed — not by notification but by the mereological fact that the whole is constituted by its parts.

item.title: Convergent Enrichment
item.description:
Convergent enrichment is a task prioritization strategy for bootstrapping a self-describing system — one where the infrastructure needed to plan work IS the work being planned. The strategy: enrich from both ends simultaneously and let the two fronts meet in the middle.

¶The problem

A self-describing system uses its own data to plan, dispatch, and track work. But when the system’s data is thin — when entities have too few predicates for the algebra to compute meaningful results — the system cannot intelligently plan its own enrichment. Querying “what should be richer?” returns ⊥ (unknown) because the predicates that would answer that question are themselves missing. The snake eats its own tail.

item.title: Failed
item.description:
Failed is the terminal state of unsuccessful work. The task’s goal became impossible or was not achieved. Unlike blocked, failed is terminal — the work will not resume.

A failed part contributes incomplete to its container. A plan with a failed task may itself fail (if the task was necessary) or may continue (if the task was not on the critical path and the plan can adapt). Whether plan failure follows from task failure depends on the plan’s structure, not on a propagation rule.

item.title: In Progress
item.description:
In-progress is the state of a unit of work that an agent is actively performing. In Aristotle’s terms, this is energeia — the actualization of the capacity that the open state held as dunamis. The work is happening.

In-progress follows open via an act of beginning (assign, start). It precedes completed via an act of finishing, failed via an act of failing, or blocked via an act of blocking.

item.title: Open
item.description:
Open is the state of a unit of work whose preconditions are met but which no agent has begun. An open task is available — it can be picked up. An open plan has been adopted but no tasks within it are active.

Open follows from either initial creation (a new task starts open) or from unblocking (a previously blocked task whose dependencies are now met returns to open). Open precedes in-progress — the transition happens when an act of beginning occurs.

A formality level measures how precisely a work specification is stated — how much of the specification is machine-checkable versus implicit or narrative. The scale runs from F0 (nothing stated) to F3 (fully algebraic).

Level Name Character Example
F3 Algebraic Machine-checkable. Typed inputs and outputs, formal invariants, decidable correctness. A tool allowlist. A double-entry ledger constraint (debits = credits). An exit code contract.
F2 Structured prose Lists, enumerations, templates with clear patterns that could be formalized. A duty checklist. Frontmatter schemas. An enumerated set of allowed actions.
F1 Narrative Natural language descriptions that resist formalization without loss. Judgment-dependent. An architect’s design rationale. A research question. A triage decision.
F0 Implicit Not stated anywhere — inferred from usage patterns, convention, or training data. The unwritten understanding that a particular agent “just knows” how to handle edge cases.

The scale originates from analyzing organizational work units — specifically, decomposing each unit into its site (action domain), recognition fibers (what it accepts), production fibers (what it produces), constructors (what operations it can perform), and invariants (what constraints it maintains). Each component of a work unit sits at some formality level independently. A unit might have F3 constructors (a typed tool list) but F1 invariants (prose principles about when to escalate).

Progressive automation is the principle that every completed task should produce not just its output but the means by which a less capable agent can replicate it. Work that required expert judgment the first time should require less judgment the second time, and eventually none.

The mechanism is encoding. When an agent solves a problem through inference — reading context, weighing options, making judgment calls — the solution path gets captured as a reusable artifact: a skill file, a script, a runbook, a hook, a frontmatter pattern. The artifact encodes the judgment so that future executions can follow the encoded path rather than re-deriving it.

A settlement path is the trajectory by which a piece of work moves from open inquiry to deterministic process. As questions get answered and methods get encoded, two dimensions settle independently: what the work produces (its meaning) and how it produces it (its execution). The path through these two dimensions defines the settlement.

The four work types mark positions on this path:

Execution settled Execution open
Meaning settled Process Procedure
Meaning open Derivation Inquiry

Settlement is not a single step but a series of transitions. A research question (inquiry) produces a finding that settles the meaning, yielding a derivation. Encoding the derivation into a conditional workflow settles the execution under known conditions, yielding a procedure. Removing the remaining conditions — handling all edge cases, formalizing all branch points — yields a process.

item.title: Capital
item.description:
Capital is a field-specific accumulated resource that generates further returns when deployed. Not raw quantity — structured, convertible asset whose value is constituted by the field in which it operates.

¶Four invariants

Something is capital if and only if it satisfies:

  1. Field-specificity. Capital has value because the field’s structure recognizes it. A university degree is capital in the academic field; it may be worthless in a street market. The field determines what counts as capital. Bourdieu: capital is “energy that only exists and only produces its effects in the field in which it is produced and reproduced.”

item.title: Availability
item.description:
Availability is the set of intervals during which an agent is able and willing to work. It is a constraint, not a commitment — being available does not mean you are assigned; it means you can be assigned. Availability is the temporal precondition for dispatch: no work can be assigned to an agent outside their available hours, regardless of capacity or organizational need.

Availability has multiple dimensions. Temporal availability specifies which hours and days the agent can work: Monday through Friday, 9am to 5pm; or overnight shifts; or on-call windows. Capability availability specifies which roles the agent is qualified to fill during those hours — a junior agent may be available full-time but only qualified for certain task classes. Energy availability acknowledges that not all available hours are equal: the same agent has higher cognitive availability in the morning than at 11pm, but may still be technically available. Goldratt (1997) treats availability as the binding constraint in multi-resource systems — if two tasks need the same agent, neither can be scheduled when the other is running, regardless of dependency structure.

item.title: Calendar
item.description:
A calendar is a system for organizing time into named, countable units — days, weeks, months, years, fiscal quarters, project phases. The calendar is the coordinate system that makes scheduling possible: without a shared calendar, “next Tuesday” means nothing to people operating in different frameworks.

A calendar is not a schedule. A calendar names time slots and provides the referent system. A schedule assigns work units to those slots. A roster assigns people to those slots. The calendar is foundational infrastructure; the schedule is allocation; the roster is human commitment. A schedule cannot exist without a calendar to define its intervals.

item.title: Captain
item.description:
A captain is the person invested with operational command of a ship — the holder of the ship’s command office, bearing absolute authority aboard within the bounds of the ship’s charter. The captain’s authority is delegated, bounded, and revocable: delegated by the commissioning authority that chartered the ship, bounded by the charter’s terms, and revocable by the commissioning authority without dissolving the ship.

The word comes from Late Latin capitaneus, from caput (head). The captain is the head of the venture — the single point of operational decision. This is not metaphor. In admiralty law, the master of a vessel holds authority that no other person aboard can override, countermand, or share. Thomas Joseph Schoenbaum’s Admiralty and Maritime Law (5th ed., 2011) states the doctrine plainly: the master’s authority is “absolute and unqualified” in matters of navigation, safety, and the prosecution of the voyage. The crew obeys; the passengers comply; the cargo is the master’s charge.

item.title: Coverage
item.description:
Coverage is the organizational invariant that ensures every required interval has at least one assigned agent responsible for a function. It answers the question: “Is someone responsible for this right now?” If the answer is ever “no” for a critical function, coverage has failed. Coverage is the constraint; shifts, watches, and rosters are the mechanisms that satisfy it.

Coverage requirements vary by function. Continuous coverage means 24/7 staffing with no gaps — hospital ICU, ship’s bridge, network operations centers. Continuous coverage requires a watch-like structure with explicit handoffs and no moment where the position is vacant. Business-hours coverage staffs only specific intervals — 9am to 5pm, weekdays only. Gaps outside covered hours are acceptable and expected. On-call coverage means not actively staffed, but someone is reachable and can respond within a defined window: cheaper than continuous but slower to respond. Surge coverage adds temporary capacity during peaks — normal staffing plus the ability to scale up during incidents or demand spikes.

item.title: Cycle
item.description: A cycle is one complete iteration of a recurrence pattern — a single turn from start through all phases back to the starting structural condition. The distinction matters precisely: recurrence names the pattern itself (the fact that reviews happen); a cycle is one instance of that pattern (this week’s review). A fiscal year that runs January through December is a recurrence. The fiscal year that just closed is one cycle of that recurrence.

item.title: Duration
item.description:
Duration is the span from the beginning to the end of a process, activity, or state — the extent of something in time, measured either as clock intervals or as lived, phenomenological experience.

Duration has two irreducible senses. Henri Bergson (1889) distinguishes durée — lived duration, the continuous unfolding of becoming, the qualitative melody that cannot be broken into discrete instants — from clock time, which is spatialized, discrete, and reversible. A task that takes “three days” in the calendar sense may have a very different durée: the experience of waiting, the subjective stretching or compression of time under stress, the intervals of absorbed focus punctuated by breaks. Both are real. Operational planning requires measured duration; understanding why estimates fail requires lived duration.

item.title: Effort
item.description:
Effort is the active work time an agent spends on a task, measured in hours, person-days, or story points. It is strictly distinct from duration — the wall-clock span from task start to finish — and from capacity — the total available work time an agent or system has in a period.

A task with three days of duration but only six hours of effort has a low effort-to-duration ratio. The gap reveals waiting time (task blocked on dependencies, waiting for information or approvals), context-switching (agent pulled away to other tasks), overhead (meetings, coordination, documentation), and idle time (agent available but the next subtask not ready). The ratio of effort to duration is flow efficiency: effort ÷ duration. Lean manufacturing, following Taiichi Ohno (1988), targets flow efficiency above 25%; knowledge work typically runs 5–15% because interruption and coordination costs are high.

item.title: Horizon
item.description:
A horizon is the temporal boundary beyond which planning, concern, or accountability does not extend. It is not a deadline (a specific point in time) but a limit of visibility — how far into the future the organization or agent is responsible for considering. You can see and plan within your horizon. Beyond it, the future is not invisible, but it is not your problem to solve right now.

Horizons exist at every scale. A task has a session or daily horizon — what can I finish in this work period? A plan has a weekly or monthly horizon — what commitments must complete by this date? A project has a quarterly or yearly horizon — where should we be at the end of this cycle? An endeavor has a generational horizon — what world are we building for our children and grandchildren? These are not separate concepts but instances of the same pattern at different scales.

item.title: Interval
item.description:
An interval is a bounded stretch of time with a definite beginning and end. It is the fundamental temporal unit: the minimal structure required to speak of when something happens, how long something lasts, or when something must be done.

Intervals require two points. The start is the moment the interval opens. The end is the moment it closes. Between them, all events that occur do so “during” the interval. This simple structure is the foundation of all temporal reasoning in scheduling. Without intervals, you cannot specify a schedule (which maps tasks to time intervals) or a deadline (which marks the end point of an interval by which work must complete).

item.title: Lifecycle
item.description:
A lifecycle is the ordered sequence of phases an entity passes through from creation to completion or dissolution. It is the temporal shape of an entity’s existence — the path it must follow from beginning to end.

A lifecycle names the states an entity occupies and specifies which transitions between states are valid. A task has a lifecycle: open → active → completed (or failed, or abandoned). A plan has a lifecycle: draft → active → completed → archived. A norm has a lifecycle: proposed → enacted → in force → amended → repealed. A role has a lifecycle: vacant → invested → occupied → relieved → vacant. The lifecycle of each is different, but the pattern is the same: an ordered path with valid transitions and terminal states.

item.title: Phase
item.description:
A phase is a distinct stage within a lifecycle — a bounded period during which an entity has a particular character and defined entry and exit conditions. A phase is one period in the temporal arc of an entity’s existence.

A phase is a state understood temporally. A state is a snapshot at a single moment — the configuration of all conditions defining a thing right now. A phase is a state that persists over time. The “in review” phase takes hours or days. The “draft” phase has a different character than the “published” phase. The phase is not just the configuration but the duration of that configuration. Three conditions define a phase: entry conditions (what must be true to enter), the character of the phase itself (what the entity is like while in this phase), and exit conditions (what must be true to leave).

item.title: Priority
item.description:
Priority is the ranking of work items by importance — which tasks matter more to the goals of the endeavor. It answers the question “how much does this matter?” High-priority work advances the mission; low-priority work is incidental. Priority is not intrinsic to the task; it is a relation between the task and the endeavor’s current needs and goals.

Priority is distinct from urgency. Priority asks “how much does this matter?” Urgency asks “how soon must this happen?” Stephen Covey (1989) formalized this distinction in the Eisenhower matrix: do first (urgent + important), schedule (important + not-urgent), delegate (urgent + not-important), drop (neither). The critical failure mode in any dispatch system is conflating the two — doing what screams loudest instead of what matters most. A fire alarm is urgent; a house with poor insulation in winter is important. A deadline creates urgency, but priority determines whether the deadline should exist at all. A trivial task with a tight deadline is still trivial; a critical task with a distant deadline is still critical.

item.title: Recurrence
item.description:
Recurrence is the temporal pattern of events that return. A ceremony that happens yearly is a recurrence. A term-limited office whose holder cycles through the community is a recurrence. An obligation that passes to descendants is a recurrence. What makes these recurrences is that the same pattern — the ceremony, the office, the obligation — appears again and again across time.

But recurrence is not repetition. Repetition is the return of identical copies: the same event, unchanged, redundant. Recurrence is the return of the same pattern in changed circumstances. This distinction matters fundamentally. Ilya Prigogine (1997) argued that time has an irreversible arrow — that forward in time means genuinely new states become possible. A spring returns to the same position in the year, but the spring that returns is spring in a world that has aged, where climate has shifted, where seeds are different. The cycle is real; the novelty is also real. The pattern spirals, not circles.

item.title: Roster
item.description:
A roster is a mapping from shifts to people. Where a schedule specifies when work must happen (8am–4pm Monday), a roster specifies who. The schedule is abstract — it names the temporal structure. The roster is concrete — it names the person accountable in that interval. A roster turns the schedule into human commitment.

A roster must satisfy five simultaneous constraints. Coverage — every shift is filled by at least one qualified person. A night shift cannot start without someone assigned to it. Availability — no one is scheduled when they cannot work. Vacation days, school hours, sleep needs, and legal time-off all forbid assignment. Fairness — undesirable shifts (nights, weekends, holidays) are distributed equitably across the staff. One person absorbs all night shifts; the organization experiences rebellion. Competency — each shift has people qualified for the role. Assigning an untrained operator to a critical system violates this constraint. Legal — labor laws and collective agreements constrain consecutive hours, mandate rest periods, and regulate shift transitions. A 7-day, 16-hour stretch may be operationally tempting; it is often legally forbidden.

item.title: Rotation
item.description:
Rotation is the recurrence of assignment: different people take the same role in sequence, and the pattern repeats across time. The duty officer rotates weekly. The on-call engineer rotates nightly. The committee chair rotates annually. The Roman consul’s term rotated every year, guaranteed by law. What makes these rotations is that the role persists (officer, engineer, chair, consul) while the occupant changes according to a fixed schedule.

Rotation serves three distinct structural purposes. First, it distributes burden: no single person bears a difficult or consuming duty permanently. A nurse on permanent night shift faces documented physiological and social costs; nurses who rotate through shifts keep their wellbeing longer. Second, it builds skill breadth: everyone learns every role. An organization where all engineers learn on-call eventually has a robust capability; an organization where one person monopolizes on-call knowledge becomes brittle when that person leaves. Third, and most politically crucial, it prevents power accumulation. A person in the same office for decades can build networks, leverage, and constituencies that become personal rather than institutional. The Athenian democracy understood this: most offices were filled by lottery (sortition) and rotated out after one year. Mogens Herman Hansen (2005) demonstrates that term limits combined with rotation made it rational for Athenian officials to honor the constitution—they knew they would soon be ordinary citizens subject to the laws they had made.

item.title: Shift
item.description:
A shift is an interval of time during which a specific person or role is assigned to cover a specific duty or function. It is the basic building block of continuous or rotating work — the bridge between the abstract schedule and the concrete commitment of a human being to show up and work.

A shift has four essential components: a start time (when responsibility begins), an end time (when responsibility ends), a role or duty (what must be covered: cashier, nurse, operator, guard), and a person assigned (the agent who will perform it). When a shift starts, that person becomes accountable. When it ends, they transfer accountability to the next shift-holder in a handoff. The shift makes abstract scheduling tangible: instead of “we need cashier coverage on Tuesday,” it becomes “Jordan covers Tuesday 9am–5pm as the cashier.”

item.title: Ship
item.description:
A ship is a chartered venture — a bounded undertaking commissioned by a higher authority, given a specific purpose, crewed for that purpose, and placed under a captain’s command. The ship’s identity comes from its charter and its flag, not from its crew or its captain. Crews rotate, captains are relieved, but the ship persists as long as the charter is in force.

The metaphor is maritime but the structure is general. In admiralty law, a ship is a legal entity distinct from its owner, its master, and its crew. The ship itself can be sued (in rem jurisdiction), can hold liens, and has a legal personality that survives changes in ownership and command. William Tetley’s International Maritime and Admiralty Law (2002) traces this doctrine to medieval Mediterranean practice: the ship was the venture, and the venture had rights and obligations independent of the persons aboard. This legal personality is what makes the ship more than a vehicle — it is an institutional entity.

item.title: Time-Block
item.description:
A time-block is an interval on a calendar reserved for specific work. “9:00 to 11:00: draft the plan.” “Monday afternoon: code review.” The time-block turns intention into commitment by occupying calendar space — that interval is now spoken for. It is the unit of intentional time allocation: the smallest structural element that converts a priority into an executable schedule.

A time-block differs from a shift in purpose and scope. A shift assigns a person to a role for a time interval — the organization needs coverage. A time-block assigns a specific piece of work to an interval on a person’s calendar — the person needs focus. Shifts are about coverage; time-blocks are about work. A factory shift (8am–4pm) can contain multiple time-blocks (setup, production run, cleanup, handoff). A time-block is more granular and more specific than a shift.

item.title: Urgency
item.description: Urgency is the temporal pressure on action — the quality that makes something need to happen now rather than later. A task is not urgent until its deadline is near; as the deadline approaches, urgency increases. Urgency is time-dependent: a high-priority task due six months from now has zero urgency today. A low-priority task due tomorrow has high urgency. This is why a deadline is not just a constraint — it is the engine that converts abstract importance into concrete time-pressure.

item.title: Watch
item.description:
A watch is a shift where the defining constraint is that the position must never be vacant. Someone is always on watch. The coverage invariant is absolute: there is no moment when the watch is unstaffed.

The term originates in naval tradition. A ship operates continuously; a ship cannot function without someone monitoring systems, tracking position, watching for hazards. The naval solution is the watch system: divide the day into fixed-length watches (traditionally four hours, with the dog watches split into two hours to rotate the schedule so sailors do not stand the same watches forever). One watch-stander stands duty throughout the watch. At the watch boundary, there is a formal handoff — the outgoing watch-stander briefs the incoming watch-stander on the ship’s status, any ongoing concerns, and any standing orders. The change of watch is recorded in the watch log. At no point is the position vacant.

item.title: Assignment
item.description:
An assignment is the act of creating a relation between a work unit and an agent. When a task is assigned, it gains an assignee — an agent who is now responsible for advancing it. The assignment is the central act of the dispatch stage.

An assignment is not just “telling someone to do something.” It is a structured act that checks three conditions before it can succeed:

Capability — the agent can do this type of work. A code review task requires an agent with code review skills. A term-writing task requires an agent with the writing-terms skill. Capability is a quality of the agent, matched against a requirement of the task.

item.title: Audit Trail
item.description:
An audit trail is an immutable sequence of events recording who did what, when, and why. Each event captures: the actor (agent or user), the action (what was done), the subject (what was acted upon), the previous state, the new state, and optionally the justification (why this act was performed).

The audit trail is the ground truth of the operations loop. Current state is a derived view — a fold over the event log (Young 2010). You can reconstruct any entity’s state at any point in time by replaying events up to that moment. You cannot reconstruct the history from current state alone, because current state discards the path that produced it.

item.title: Backlog
item.description:
A backlog is a container of candidate work units that have been captured but not yet committed to a plan. It is the holding area between “someone thought of this” and “we’ve decided to do this.”

The backlog differs from a queue in commitment level. Items in a queue are committed — they will be done, the question is when. Items in a backlog are candidates — they might be done, promoted to a plan, deferred indefinitely, or discarded. The backlog is where inputs accumulate before the planning stage evaluates and prioritizes them.

item.title: Bottleneck
item.description:
A bottleneck is the constraint that limits the throughput of the entire system. It is not the slowest task or the busiest agent — it is the specific resource whose capacity determines how fast work flows from entry to completion. Everything upstream of the bottleneck produces faster than it can consume; everything downstream starves.

Goldratt’s Theory of Constraints (1984) provides the operational framework. The five focusing steps: (1) identify the constraint — find where work accumulates (the longest queue), (2) exploit it — ensure the constraint is never idle, never works on low-priority items, never wastes capacity on rework, (3) subordinate everything else to it — non-constraints serve the constraint’s needs, not their own optimization, (4) elevate it — invest in increasing the constraint’s capacity, (5) repeat — once this constraint is resolved, a new one emerges.

item.title: Cadence
item.description:
Cadence is the rhythm of recurring operational events — the regular beats at which the operations loop runs its stages. A daily standup. A weekly review. A monthly retrospective. A quarterly planning cycle. Cadence creates predictability: everyone knows when things happen, which reduces coordination overhead.

Cadence operates at multiple time scales simultaneously:

Scale Cadence Loop stages emphasized
Turn Per agent message Tracking (environment composition)
Session Per work session All six stages within session scope
Daily Once per day Dispatch, tracking
Weekly Once per week Review, planning
Monthly Once per month Feedback, project review
Quarterly Once per quarter Goal review, strategy adjustment

Each level feeds the one above it. Daily tracking surfaces weekly themes. Weekly reviews feed monthly assessments. The information flows upward; the direction flows downward.

item.title: Capacity
item.description:
Capacity is the amount of work an agent or system can handle in a given time period. It is a quality of the agent — not a property of the work. The same task takes different amounts of capacity from different agents depending on their skills, tools, and context.

Capacity has two dimensions: throughput capacity (how many work units can be completed per period) and concurrent capacity (how many work units can be in progress simultaneously). These are related by Little’s Law (1961): average items in system equals throughput times average time in system. An agent who can complete 3 tasks per day and handles them sequentially has a concurrent capacity of about 1. An agent who runs 5 tasks in parallel with 2-day cycle times has the same throughput but concurrent capacity of ~3.

item.title: Deadline
item.description:
A deadline is a fixed point in time by which a work unit must be completed. It converts priority (which work matters more) into urgency (which work matters now). A high-priority task with a distant deadline can wait. A low-priority task due tomorrow cannot.

Deadlines are constraints, not goals. A goal says what must be accomplished. A deadline says when. The distinction matters because a task can meet its deadline while failing its goal (rushed, low-quality completion) or miss its deadline while exceeding its goal (thorough, late completion). The tracking stage must evaluate both.

item.title: Handoff
item.description:
A handoff is the act of transferring responsibility for a work unit from one agent to another. It is not merely reassignment — it requires transferring enough context that the receiving agent can continue the work without starting over.

A handoff has three components:

Context transfer — the receiving agent must know what has been done, what remains, what was tried and failed, and what assumptions are in play. In multi-agent AI systems, context loss at handoff is the primary failure mode. An agent that picks up a task mid-stream without context will repeat work, miss nuance, or contradict earlier decisions. Cold-start executable plans (where each step carries enough context to be picked up independently) are a structural mitigation.

item.title: Metric
item.description:
A metric is a quantitative measure of a quality — a number assigned to an aspect of a thing to enable comparison, tracking, and decision-making. Task completion count, plan progress percentage, cycle time, cost per session — all metrics.

Stevens (1946) identifies four levels of measurement. A metric’s level determines which operations are meaningful:

Level Property Operations Work examples
Nominal Equality Count, mode Task type, status label
Ordinal Order Median, rank Priority, risk level
Interval Equal intervals Mean, difference Dates, sprint numbers
Ratio True zero All arithmetic Cost, duration, task count

The level matters because averaging an ordinal metric is meaningless. “Average priority” across tasks produces a number that means nothing. “Average cycle time” across tasks is meaningful because cycle time is ratio-scale.

item.title: Milestone
item.description:
A milestone is a recognizable state of a plan — a specific configuration of task completions that marks meaningful progress toward the plan’s goal. Milestones are not tasks. They are not acts. They are states — snapshots of the plan at a moment where something significant has been achieved.

A milestone serves two functions. First, it makes progress visible. A plan with 30 tasks and no milestones gives you only two data points: 0% and 100%. A plan with 5 milestones gives you 7 data points, letting you track progress continuously and detect slowdowns early.

item.title: Queue
item.description:
A queue is a container that holds work units in a defined order, awaiting dispatch or processing. Work enters the queue when it is ready to be acted on. Work leaves the queue when an agent pulls it or a dispatcher assigns it.

A queue has an ordering principle. The simplest is first-in-first-out (FIFO) — work is processed in the order it arrived. Priority queues order by urgency or value — highest-priority work comes first regardless of arrival time. Cost-of-delay queues order by economic impact — work whose delay costs the most goes first. The ordering principle is a quality of the queue, not of the work units — the same tasks can be ordered differently in different queues.

item.title: Review
item.description:
A review is the act of comparing actual outcomes against planned outcomes. It takes what happened and what was supposed to happen and produces a judgment: what went right, what went wrong, and why. Review is where the operations loop learns.

A review is not a status check. Status checks observe the current state — “where are we?” A review evaluates the trajectory — “how did we get here, and was it the right path?” The distinction matters: tracking is continuous observation, review is periodic assessment.

item.title: Schedule
item.description:
A schedule is a mapping from work units to time intervals. Where a plan specifies what must be done and in what order, a schedule specifies when. The plan says “task B depends on task A.” The schedule says “task A starts Monday, task B starts Wednesday.”

A schedule commits resources to time. Once a task is scheduled, the agent’s time in that interval is spoken for. This is why Bratman (1987) treats plans as commitments that constrain future deliberation: a schedule forecloses alternatives. If you’ve scheduled the code review for Thursday, you stop considering whether to do the code review — the question is settled, and your attention goes to what else needs to happen by Thursday.

A derivation is work whose execution path is settled but whose meaning is not. You know exactly what steps to follow. You do not know what the steps will produce until you follow them. The output is a judgment — a named, typed result whose content could not have been predicted from the input alone.

A derivation follows fixed inference rules. The rules are explicit and fully determined — there are no branches, no conditions to evaluate, no decisions to make about which rule to apply next. The rules are applied in order. But the result depends on the input in a way that cannot be shortcut: you must actually run the derivation to see what comes out.

An inquiry is work where neither the meaning nor the execution is settled. You do not fully know what you are looking for, and you do not know how to find it. The work is directed by a question, not by a specification.

Inquiry is the most open form of work. A process knows both what and how. A procedure knows what but branches on how. A derivation knows how but not yet what it will produce. An inquiry knows neither — it is exploring.

A method is the system of conventions, specifications, skills, and policies that governs how work is conducted. Method describes how things should be done. Practice is the actual doing. Method is the score; practice is the performance.

Four properties make something a method:

Codification. A method is documented — as specifications, policies, skills, or other explicit artifacts. Uncodified habit is not method. A team that “just knows how to do it” has practice but not method. Method begins when the practice is written down, and it becomes method when the documentation is treated as governing rather than merely descriptive.

A procedure is work whose meaning is settled but whose execution path is not. You know what you are trying to produce. You do not know which steps you will take to produce it, because that depends on conditions you evaluate as you go.

A procedure branches. At each decision point, it inspects the current state, evaluates a condition, and routes to the appropriate next step. The output type is fixed — every branch produces a result of the same kind. But which branch fires depends on the input and the state encountered during execution.

A process is a fully determined transformation. Given the same input, it always produces the same output. There are no decisions to make, no conditions to evaluate, no branches to take. You run it and get the result.

A process knows what it is producing (its meaning is settled) and how to produce it (its execution path is settled). This double settlement is what distinguishes a process from every other kind of work. A procedure knows what it is producing but must evaluate conditions to determine the path. An inquiry knows neither. A process knows both — it is the work that is already fully specified.

A task is a unit of work organized around a goal. It has three parts: a goal (what counts as done), a set of subtasks or operations (what must happen to get there), and a plan (the order and conditions under which subtasks execute).

The goal defines the task. The task succeeds when the goal is achieved. The task fails when the goal becomes impossible. The task is abandoned when the agent releases the goal. Without a goal, there is activity but not a task — the goal is what gives the work its boundary.

Work is an act with a defined output. It restricts the general concept of action — what a thing does that produces change — to episodes with nameable results. An act that produces nothing nameable is not work; it is time spent. Work inherits from act the structure of capacity realization: something has the potential to produce an output, and work is the episode where that potential is realized.

This is the base concept. All organized activity is work when it has a defined output. The four types of work — process, procedure, derivation, and inquiry — are specializations based on which qualities of the action are settled. Two qualities matter: whether the meaning of the output is known (meaning-settled) and whether the execution path to produce it is known (execution-settled). Process has both settled. Inquiry has neither. Procedure and derivation each have one.

A workflow is a composition of work units with one algebraic constraint: the initial input type equals the final output type. The workflow takes something of type X and produces something of type X, regardless of what happens internally. The internal structure is unconstrained — flat sequence, branching web, parallel paths, nested sub-workflows. What matters is the boundary: same type in, same type out.

This constraint is what makes workflows composable. Two workflows on the same type can be chained. A workflow can be nested inside another workflow as a single step. A workflow can replace any work unit that has the same input and output type. The algebraic boundary makes substitution safe.

An objective is a specific, measurable, time-bounded target that serves a broader goal. It has four parts: a target (what counts as achieved), a goal it serves (the broader direction), a progress metric (how to track movement), and a deadline (when it must be achieved by).

The defining property is decidability. An objective is either achieved or not — there is no ambiguity. This distinguishes it from a goal (which specifies direction but may never be fully “achieved”), an intent (which specifies how to reconstruct something but has no deadline), and a task (which has a completion condition but not necessarily a metric or deadline).

item.title: Company
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A Company is a quadruple K=(K,S,G,ℓ)\mathcal{K} = (K, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{G}, \ell):

K=(K:Charter,  S:Staff,  G:Governance,  ℓ:Standing)\mathcal{K} = (K : \mathrm{Charter},\; \mathcal{S} : \mathrm{Staff},\; \mathcal{G} : \mathrm{Governance},\; \ell : \mathrm{Standing})

where:

  • KK is the charter — the constitutive document that specifies the company’s common purpose, establishes its normative order, and declares its membership conditions; KK is not merely the vessel’s background document but the explicit formulation of what members share — the telos that makes membership meaningful and constitutes the fellowship
  • S\mathcal{S} is the staff — the ongoing personnel bearing roles within the company; unlike a crew (bounded by a voyage TT), the staff’s role-bindings persist through the company’s history without a pre-specified terminal period
  • G\mathcal{G} is the governance mechanism — the collective decision procedure that aggregates staff inputs into group-level decisions attributable to the company as a whole rather than to individual members; without G\mathcal{G}, the staff acts in parallel but the company does not act as one
  • ℓ\ell is the institutional standing — the status-function declaration of the form “this collective counts as YY in context CC” that gives the company its entity identity; ℓ\ell is what makes “the company decided” a coherent attribution; it installs the company as a recognized node in a normative system

A Company is a Vessel — it is an institutional entity bearing officers, carrying a charter, and persisting through changes in personnel. What the company adds over bare vessel:

item.title: Enrollment
item.description:
¶Formal definition

An Enrollment is a triple E=(p,V,T)\mathcal{E} = (p, V, T):

E=(p:Person,  V:Vessel,  T:Interval)\mathcal{E} = (p : \mathrm{Person},\; V : \mathrm{Vessel},\; T : \mathrm{Interval})

together with:

  • A role assignment r=R(p)∈Roles(V)r = R(p) \in \mathrm{Roles}(V) — the specific position pp fills in VV’s complement
  • An enrollment act ιp\iota_p — the discrete event (signing of articles, assumption of duty, formal assignment) at which pp becomes a section of VV’s role fiber for TT
  • A discharge predicate δp\delta_p — the condition (end of voyage, relief, death) at which the section status terminates

The enrollment constitutes pp as a section of the role fibration over VV for period TT: the assignment R(p)=rR(p) = r is a right inverse of the role projection πr:Crew(V,T)→Roles(V)\pi_r : \mathrm{Crew}(V, T) \to \mathrm{Roles}(V) at position rr. The section condition is: πr(R(p))=r\pi_r(R(p)) = r — after enrollment, pp is provably in position rr.

item.title: Enterprise
item.description:
¶Formal definition

An Enterprise is a triple E=(C,Γ,O)\mathcal{E} = (C, \Gamma, \mathcal{O}):

E=(C:HardCore,  Γ:AimSet→P(Aims),  O:OperativeStructure)\mathcal{E} = (C : \mathrm{HardCore},\; \Gamma : \mathrm{AimSet} \to \mathcal{P}(\mathrm{Aims}),\; \mathcal{O} : \mathrm{OperativeStructure})

where:

  • CC is the hard core — the structured set of constitutive commitments that are treated as non-negotiable by the enterprise’s participants; CC gives the enterprise its identity; what makes this enterprise this enterprise rather than another is its hard core; the hard core includes the enterprise’s internal goods (MacIntyre), its guiding aims, its standards of excellence, and the negative heuristic — the boundary beyond which change counts as a different enterprise rather than the same enterprise changing
  • Γ\Gamma is the generative mechanism — a function from the current aim-set AtA_t to a set of new potential aims Γ(At)⊆Aims\Gamma(A_t) \subseteq \mathrm{Aims}; the generative mechanism encodes the process by which the pursuit of current aims produces residues — idle capabilities, unresolved tensions, deepened understanding that reveals new dimensions of the goal — and those residues constitute At+1A_{t+1}; the enterprise is aim-generative iff Γ(At)≠∅\Gamma(A_t) \neq \emptyset for all tt at which the enterprise is genuinely active
  • O\mathcal{O} is the operative structure — the organized cooperative activity through which CC is pursued and Γ\Gamma is instantiated; O\mathcal{O} includes the agents, roles, vessels, authority relations, and procedures through which the enterprise operates; O\mathcal{O} may take the form of a Company K=(K,S,G,ℓ)\mathcal{K} = (K, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{G}, \ell), a looser Staff, or an informal cooperative arrangement; the operative structure is the carrier of the enterprise, but it is not the enterprise

Four invariants. E\mathcal{E} is an enterprise iff it satisfies:

item.title: Focus
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A Focus is a pair F=(H,t∗)\mathcal{F} = (H, t^*):

F=(H:Top→HAnucl,  t∗∈T)\mathcal{F} = (H : T^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathbf{HA}_{\mathrm{nucl}},\; t^* \in T)

where:

  • HH is the ambient presheaf — the RelationalState: the full indexed family of fiber Heyting algebras HtH_t over the history category TT, connected by restriction maps ρt′∣t:Ht′→Ht\rho_{t'|t} : H_{t'} \to H_t for t≤t′t \leq t', each fiber carrying the commuting nuclear pair (σt,Δt)(\sigma_t, \Delta_t)
  • t∗∈Tt^* \in T is the focal history — the designated current position in the history category; the element at which the RelationalMachine is presently operating; the fiber Ht∗H_{t^*} is what relational-state.md calls “what the RelationalMachine holds in focus at history tt” and indexed-automaton.md calls “the fiber in focus”

The focal fiber Ht∗H_{t^*} is not the whole of F\mathcal{F} — it is the theme of a tripartite field structure that the designation t∗t^* induces on HH.

item.title: Gap
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A Gap is a triple (a,σt(a),Δt(a))(a, \sigma_t(a), \Delta_t(a)):

Gapt(a)=(a,  σt(a),  Δt(a))a∈Ht\mathrm{Gap}_t(a) = (a,\; \sigma_t(a),\; \Delta_t(a)) \quad a \in H_t

where:

  • a∈Hta \in H_t is the element — the fiber element being assessed
  • σt(a)∈Fix(σt)\sigma_t(a) \in \mathrm{Fix}(\sigma_t) is the saturation-closure — the smallest saturation-nucleus-fixed element ≥a\geq a; how far aa must move to be meaning-settled
  • Δt(a)∈Fix(Δt)\Delta_t(a) \in \mathrm{Fix}(\Delta_t) is the transfer-closure — the smallest transfer-nucleus-fixed element ≥a\geq a; how far aa must move to be execution-settled

Zero-gap condition. a∈Ht∗a \in H^*_t iff σt(a)=a\sigma_t(a) = a and Δt(a)=a\Delta_t(a) = a — both gaps are zero. The element is at its own closures; neither nucleus demands more. Full settlement is the coincidence of both gaps being zero simultaneously.

item.title: MacroFocus
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A MacroFocus is a pointed presheaf Fmac=(H,t∗)\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{mac}} = (H, t^*):

Fmac=(H:Top→HAnucl,  t∗∈T)\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{mac}} = (H : T^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathbf{HA}_{\mathrm{nucl}},\; t^* \in T)

where:

  • HH is the ambient presheaf — the full RelationalState: the family of fiber Heyting algebras (Ht)t∈T(H_t)_{t \in T} connected by restriction maps ρt′∣t:Ht′→Ht\rho_{t'|t} : H_{t'} \to H_t for t≤t′t \leq t'; each fiber carries the commuting nuclear pair (σt,Δt)(\sigma_t, \Delta_t)
  • t∗∈Tt^* \in T is the focal history — the designated current position in the history category TT

A MacroFocus IS a Focus — the pair (H,t∗)(H, t^*) is identical. MacroFocus names this structure at Level 1 of the three-level focus stack: the fiber-level designation. It answers the question: which fiber is the current working context? The answer is Ht∗H_{t^*}.

item.title: MesoFocus
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A MesoFocus is a triple Fmes=(Ht∗,j,Fix(j))\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{mes}} = (H_{t^*}, j, \mathrm{Fix}(j)):

Fmes=(Ht∗:HAnucl,  j:Ht∗→Ht∗,  Fix(j)⊆Ht∗)\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{mes}} = (H_{t^*} : \mathbf{HA}_{\mathrm{nucl}},\; j : H_{t^*} \to H_{t^*},\; \mathrm{Fix}(j) \subseteq H_{t^*})

where:

  • Ht∗H_{t^*} is the focal fiber — the Heyting algebra obtained from a MacroFocus (H,t∗)(H, t^*) at the designated history t∗t^*; it carries the relational universe’s nuclear pair (σt∗,Δt∗)(\sigma_{t^*}, \Delta_{t^*})
  • j:Ht∗→Ht∗j : H_{t^*} \to H_{t^*} is the focal nucleus — an internal nuclear endomorphism on Ht∗H_{t^*}: idempotent (j∘j=jj \circ j = j), monotone (a≤b⇒j(a)≤j(b)a \leq b \Rightarrow j(a) \leq j(b)), inflationary (a≤j(a)a \leq j(a)), and a Heyting algebra endomorphism (j(a∧b)=j(a)∧j(b)j(a \wedge b) = j(a) \wedge j(b))
  • Fix(j)={a∈Ht∗:j(a)=a}\mathrm{Fix}(j) = \{a \in H_{t^*} : j(a) = a\} is the focal sublocale — the sub-Heyting-algebra of elements already at their jj-closure; in locale theory, this is the closed sublocale defined by jj; it is the “settled” region of Ht∗H_{t^*} relative to the nucleus jj

A MesoFocus presupposes a MacroFocus: the fiber Ht∗H_{t^*} exists only after t∗t^* has been designated. A MesoFocus does not refine the designation t∗t^* — it operates within the already-designated fiber. It answers: what is settled here?

item.title: MicroFocus
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A MicroFocus is a pair Fmic=(Ht∗,a∗)\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{mic}} = (H_{t^*}, a^*):

Fmic=(Ht∗:HAnucl,  a∗∈Ht∗)\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{mic}} = (H_{t^*} : \mathbf{HA}_{\mathrm{nucl}},\; a^* \in H_{t^*})

where:

  • Ht∗H_{t^*} is the focal fiber — the Heyting algebra obtained from a MacroFocus (H,t∗)(H, t^*); it carries the commuting nuclear pair (σt∗,Δt∗)(\sigma_{t^*}, \Delta_{t^*})
  • a∗∈Ht∗a^* \in H_{t^*} is the focal element — the specific designated proposition currently under active processing; not a set, not a sublocale, but a single element of the fiber

A MicroFocus presupposes a MacroFocus: Ht∗H_{t^*} exists only after t∗t^* has been designated. A MicroFocus does not presuppose a MesoFocus: element designation does not require prior settlement classification, though the relationship between a∗a^* and Fix(j)\mathrm{Fix}(j) is operationally significant (see below).

item.title: Plan
item.description:
A plan is a structured commitment to a finite set of intended actions, connected by dependencies, aimed at transforming a current situation toward a goal. A plan says what must happen and what must happen before what — but it does not dictate every detail of execution.

¶What makes something a plan

Four properties distinguish a plan from a wish, a list, or a policy:

item.title: Project
item.description:
A project is a named container of related plans oriented toward a single stated goal. It is the highest-level grouping of work below organizational goals — a project says “this is the thing we are trying to accomplish” and the plans within it say how.

¶What makes something a project

A project has three defining properties:

  1. A stated goal. The goal says what success looks like when the project is complete. Without a goal, a project is just a label on a pile of work — there is no way to know when it is finished.

item.title: Refocusing
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A Refocusing is a morphism R:(H,t∗)→(H′,t′)\mathfrak{R} : (H, t^*) \to (H', t') in the category of pointed presheaves:

R=(ρ:H→H′,  ϕ:t∗⇝t′)\mathfrak{R} = (\rho : H \to H',\; \phi : t^* \leadsto t')

where:

  • (H,t∗)(H, t^*) is the source MacroFocus — the presheaf H:Top→HAnuclH : T^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathbf{HA}_{\mathrm{nucl}} with designated history t∗t^*
  • (H′,t′)(H', t') is the target MacroFocus — the presheaf H′H' (possibly equal to HH) with the new designated history t′t'
  • ρ:H→H′\rho : H \to H' is a presheaf morphism — a natural transformation between the ambient presheaves; when H′=HH' = H, this is idH\mathrm{id}_H
  • ϕ:t∗⇝t′\phi : t^* \leadsto t' is the history reassignment — the record of which history is now focal; in the forward extension case, ϕ\phi is the step morphism s⋆t∗=t′s \star t^* = t'; in the backward and lateral cases, ϕ\phi is a morphism in TT or a choice from TT

Three invariants. R\mathfrak{R} is a refocusing iff:

item.title: Runbook
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A Runbook is a four-tuple R=(N,R,Π,G)\mathcal{R} = (\mathcal{N}, R, \Pi, \mathcal{G}):

R=(N:Norm,  R:Role,  Π=[a1,…,an]:Act∗,  G:Goal)\mathcal{R} = (\mathcal{N} : \mathrm{Norm},\; R : \mathrm{Role},\; \Pi = [a_1, \ldots, a_n] : \mathrm{Act}^*,\; \mathcal{G} : \mathrm{Goal})

where:

  • N=(ψ,Obl,I)\mathcal{N} = (\psi, \mathrm{Obl}, \mathcal{I}) is the trigger norm — the norm whose activation initiates the runbook; ψ\psi is the propositional content; when ψ∈Ht∗\psi \in H^*_t, the runbook is activated
  • RR is the executor role — the role whose incumbent is obligated to execute Π\Pi when N\mathcal{N} fires; the incumbent need not have designed Π\Pi, only be qualified to execute it
  • Π=[a1,…,an]\Pi = [a_1, \ldots, a_n] is the step sequence — an ordered list of acts; Π∈Fix(Δt)\Pi \in \mathrm{Fix}(\Delta_t) at all tt for which R\mathcal{R} is valid; the sequence is execution-settled before any trigger fires; the executor exercises no substantive judgment about what the steps are
  • G=(g,τ,κ)\mathcal{G} = (g, \tau, \kappa) is the goal — the target element g∈Ht∖Ht∗g \in H_t \setminus H^*_t that Π\Pi drives to Hτ∗H^*_\tau; execution terminates when g∈Hτ∗g \in H^*_\tau

The output of executing R\mathcal{R} from history t0t_0 is the morphism f:t0→τf : t_0 \to \tau in TT tracing the path t0⋆φ1⋆⋯⋆φn=τt_0 \star \varphi_1 \star \cdots \star \varphi_n = \tau, where each φi∈Hti\varphi_i \in H_{t_i} is the fiber element produced by act aia_i.

item.title: Staff
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A Staff is a quintuple S=(V,P,R,σ,M)\mathcal{S} = (V, P, R, \sigma, M):

S=(V:Vessel,  P:Set(Person),  R⊆Roles(V),  σ:P→P(R),  M:Mandate)\mathcal{S} = (V : \mathrm{Vessel},\; P : \mathrm{Set(Person)},\; R \subseteq \mathrm{Roles}(V),\; \sigma : P \to \mathcal{P}(R),\; M : \mathrm{Mandate})

where:

  • VV is the vessel — the institutional entity whose positions the staff fills; VV determines the role structure Roles(V)\mathrm{Roles}(V), the authority structure on those roles, and the charter that generates MM
  • PP is the personnel — the finite set of persons who are staff members; a person is in the staff iff they bear at least one role: p∈P⇔σ(p)≠∅p \in P \Leftrightarrow \sigma(p) \neq \emptyset
  • R⊆Roles(V)R \subseteq \mathrm{Roles}(V) is the staffed role set — the subset of the vessel’s roles that are currently filled by staff members; RR need not cover all of Roles(V)\mathrm{Roles}(V) (there may be vacant positions), but a fully-staffed vessel has R=Roles(V)R = \mathrm{Roles}(V)
  • σ:P→P(R)\sigma : P \to \mathcal{P}(R) is the role-assignment function — mapping each staff member to the (possibly multiple) roles they bear; the assignment creates the institutional binding: r∈σ(p)r \in \sigma(p) means “person pp bears role rr in vessel VV”
  • MM is the functional mandate — the shared operational purpose that orients all of PP and gives the staff its unity; MM is derived from VV’s charter and specifies what the staff collectively exists to do

A Staff is not a crew. The defining difference is duration: crew status is bounded by an operational period TT (voyage, mission, flight — a specific interval with a start and end), while staff status is ongoing — it persists through the vessel’s history without a pre-specified terminal period. The same person may be staff when assigned to a vessel’s permanent complement and crew when assigned to operate that vessel on a specific voyage.

item.title: Clerk
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A Clerk is a six-tuple K=(P,D,R,A,M,X)\mathcal{K} = (P, D, R, A, M, X):

K=(P:Institution,  D:DecisionMaker,  R:Record,  A:R→R∗,  M:Mandate,  X⊆A)\mathcal{K} = (P : \mathrm{Institution},\; D : \mathrm{DecisionMaker},\; R : \mathrm{Record},\; A : R \to R^*,\; M : \mathrm{Mandate},\; X \subseteq A)

where:

  • PP is the principal institution — the court, house, council, or company whose memory the clerk maintains; the clerk is accountable to PP as a whole, not exclusively to any individual within PP
  • DD is the decision-maker — the judge, parliament, board, or sovereign whose decisions the clerk records; DD makes the decision; K\mathcal{K} records it; these are distinct acts
  • RR is the official record — the docket, roll, minute book, or register of which K\mathcal{K} has exclusive authorized custody; RR is PP’s institutional memory
  • A:R→R∗A : R \to R^* is the authentication function — the clerk’s signature, seal, certification, or pronouncement that transforms a document r∈Rr \in R into an officially valid record r∗=A(r)∈R∗r^* = A(r) \in R^*; without A(r)A(r), the document is a draft
  • MM is the ministerial mandate — the prescribed procedures, rules, and statutes governing when and how K\mathcal{K} must apply AA; MM admits no substantive discretion; refusal to apply AA when MM demands it is itself unlawful
  • X⊆AX \subseteq A is the non-delegable authentication core — the specific acts within AA that cannot be sub-delegated without formal authorization; when authorized, the delegate acts in K\mathcal{K}’s formal capacity, not their own

Seven invariants. K\mathcal{K} is a clerk iff it satisfies:

item.title: Delegation
item.description:
A delegation is the transfer of bounded authority from a principal to a beneficiary, where accountability remains with the principal. The principal can give the beneficiary the power to act; the principal cannot give the beneficiary the duty to answer for outcomes.

¶Four invariants

A delegation has four structural properties:

  1. Derivativity. The principal can only delegate what the principal holds. You cannot give authority you do not have. The Latin maxim: nemo dat quod non habet — no one gives what they do not have.

item.title: Endeavor
item.description:
An endeavor is an organized, sustained, failure-open striving toward an aim, commissioned by the striver. It has five parts:

  1. Committing agent — the person or unified group who takes up the endeavor. Not a participant but the locus of commitment. Without a determinate committer, there is activity but not endeavor.
  2. Directed aim — the orientation toward which effort is directed. Specific enough to distinguish striving from drifting, but the aim need not be fully specified in advance. You can endeavor to “understand physics” — an aim with no crisp terminal deliverable.
  3. Self-commitment — the agent’s placement of themselves in quasi-positional obligation toward the aim. Self-generated, no external sender required. This is what distinguishes endeavor from mandate or duty.
  4. Sustained exertion — the temporal structure: not a single act but a structure unfolding across multiple episodes, attempts, setbacks, and adjustments. A single try is an attempt. Sustained committed trying is an endeavor.
  5. Failure-openness — the aim is not guaranteed by the endeavoring. The endeavor can succeed, fail, or be abandoned. Without this, the activity is a procedure, not an endeavor.

¶Properties

Self-commissioning. No external sender is required. The commitment is self-generated. This distinguishes endeavor from a mission (which requires being commissioned) and from a duty (which derives from a position in a normative system). You can endeavor without being sent.

item.title: Incumbent
item.description:
¶What it is

An Incumbent is a quadruple (O,  tnow,  a,  ι)(O,\; t_{\mathrm{now}},\; a,\; \iota):

Inc=(O,  tnow∈T,  a:Entity,  ι∈Htnow)\mathrm{Inc} = (O,\; t_{\mathrm{now}} \in T,\; a : \text{Entity},\; \iota \in H_{t_{\mathrm{now}}})

where:

  • O=(T,J,H,σ,Δ)O = (T, J, H, \sigma, \Delta) is the Office — the normative presheaf with nuclei defined over all histories in TT
  • tnow∈Tt_{\mathrm{now}} \in T is the current history — the designated base point for “now”; incumbency is a relation at a specific history, not a global structure
  • aa is the occupant — an Entity whose id is a doubly-stable proposition in H∗H^*; the natural person currently wearing the body politic
  • ι∈Htnow\iota \in H_{t_{\mathrm{now}}} is the incumbency proposition — the element of the fiber Heyting algebra asserting “aa holds OO at tnowt_{\mathrm{now}}”

Type note. The occupant aa is an Entity (a named individual — a section of an agent-sheaf with a doubly-stable id), not itself an element of HtnowH_{t_{\mathrm{now}}}. The fiber Heyting algebra HtH_t carries propositions, not individuals (Lambek–Scott 1986, Introduction to Higher Order Categorical Logic; Johnstone 2002, Sketches of an Elephant, Part D). The incumbency proposition ι∈Htnow\iota \in H_{t_{\mathrm{now}}} is the proposition about aa; aa is the individual the proposition names. What the original three-component tuple conflated — occupant individual and occupancy truth value — are now distinct.

item.title: Office
item.description:
An office is a normative position that exists and persists independently of any particular holder. The structural fact from which everything else follows: the authority of an office belongs to the office, not to the person who occupies it.

When a person is installed in an office, she acquires the office’s authority for the duration of her occupancy. When she vacates, the authority does not dissolve — it remains in the office, waiting for the next holder. This is the medieval legal doctrine dignitas non moritur — “the dignity does not die” (Kantorowicz, The King’s Two Bodies, 1957, p. 383).

item.title: Secretary
item.description:
¶Formal definition

A Secretary is a five-tuple E=(Q,R,C,F,κ)\mathcal{E} = (Q, R, C, F, \kappa):

E=(Q:Body,  R⊆Records,  C:Decisions(Q)→OfficialCorrespondence,  F:R→Repository,  κ:R→R∗)\mathcal{E} = (Q : \mathrm{Body},\; R \subseteq \mathrm{Records},\; C : \mathrm{Decisions}(Q) \to \mathrm{Official Correspondence},\; F : R \to \mathrm{Repository},\; \kappa : R \to R^*)

where:

  • QQ is the principal body — the decision-making authority (board, legislature, executive, meeting) whose decisions the secretary records and transmits; QQ makes decisions; E\mathcal{E} records and authenticates them
  • RR is the record set — the official documents over which the secretary has custody: minutes, registers, resolutions, official filings, statutory books; E\mathcal{E} is the custodian of RR
  • CC is the communications function — the map from QQ’s decisions to official outward transmissions; the secretary is the official channel through which QQ’s decisions are communicated externally
  • FF is the filing obligation — the requirement to deposit RR into the appropriate repository (public register, official archive, regulatory body) within required timeframes
  • κ\kappa is the authentication function — the secretary’s signature, seal, or attestation that transforms a document r∈Rr \in R into an officially valid record r∗=κ(r)∈R∗r^* = \kappa(r) \in R^*; without κ(r)\kappa(r), the document is a draft

Five invariants. E\mathcal{E} is a secretary iff it satisfies:

item.title: Serving
item.description:
¶What this is

Serving is the act of occupying a position within a structured system and fulfilling the obligations of that position. It connects an Officer to a Vessel through an endeavor.

¶Formal definition

Serving is stratified into three levels of increasing structural obligation. Each level is a formal object:

Level 1 — Structural occupancy.

Serving1=(p:E→B,  s:B→E,  p∘s=idB)\text{Serving}_1 = (p : E \to B,\; s : B \to E,\; p \circ s = \mathrm{id}_B)

A section: a morphism ss choosing one element per fiber, satisfying the section condition. The servant is in the right place.

item.title: Steward
item.description:
¶What this is

A Steward (oikonomos, οἰκονόμος) is an agent who manages another’s household, estate, or domain by delegated authority.

The term combines oikos (house, household, estate) with nemein (to manage, to distribute, to govern). The oikonomos in classical Greek usage is the household administrator: not the owner, not a slave executing commands, but the manager who has been entrusted with full operational authority over the estate in the owner’s name and for the owner’s benefit.

item.title: OperationItem
item.description:
¶What this is

An OperationItem is the carrier of the initial algebra for the functor F(X) = WorkUnit ⊕ List(X).

The mathematical invariant comes from the theory of initial algebras and algebraic data types (Lambek 1968; Hagino 1987). Given a functor F: Set → Set, an F-algebra is a pair (A, α: F(A) → A). An initial F-algebra is one from which there is a unique algebra homomorphism to every other F-algebra — it is the canonical recursive type defined by F.

item.title: Work Item
item.description:
A work item is an element of a task. It is either:

  • Atomic: a work unit — concrete, executable, producing a single named output
  • Composite: a workflow — a structured composition of work items with defined inputs and outputs

This makes work item a recursive type: a workflow is itself a work item, so tasks can contain both atoms and compounds at any depth.

¶What makes something a work item

A work item is a piece of work that has been judged actionable and placed somewhere trackable — a queue, a backlog, or a plan. The key transition: raw input (an idea, a request, a problem noticed) becomes a work item when someone decides “this needs doing” and commits it to the system.

item.title: Work Unit
item.description:
A work unit is a concrete, fully specified instance of work. All parameters are bound to specific values: who does it, what they do it to, what operation they perform, and what output they produce. Nothing is left open.

¶What makes something a work unit

The defining property is complete specification. A work unit names:

  • What goes in — the specific input or material being worked on
  • What operation is performed — the specific transformation applied
  • What comes out — the specific output produced
  • What parameters govern execution — the concrete values that distinguish this instance from any other

A task like “review the dispatch survey” is a work unit: the input (dispatch survey), the operation (review), and the expected output (review findings) are all specified. A task like “improve the documentation” is not a work unit — it leaves the operation and scope open.

A practice is the actual doing of work — activity conducted according to method. Diagnosing a patient, filing a report, reviewing a pull request, standing a watch, conducting an interview — these are practices. They have rules (written or implicit), roles (who does what), settings (where they happen), and products (what they generate).

Three properties make something a practice:

Activity. A practice is something done, not something described. Practice is action, not documentation. The method describes how to do it; the practice is the doing. You can read about surgery without practicing surgery. You can write a deployment checklist without deploying.

item.title: Skill
item.description: A skill is compiled procedural knowledge — a named, repeatable unit of method that an agent can invoke reliably. In ACT-R and cognitive science, a skill is procedural memory that runs without deliberation: the agent does not reason about how to do it, it just does it. In robotics, a skill is the middle layer: parameterized, reusable, composed into task plans by a higher-level planner. In AI agent frameworks, a skill is a named capability with defined preconditions and effects.
